comment_text.sn41.7:1.3: This question was asked by Mahākoṭṭhita of Sāriputta at MN 43:29.5, and by Pañcakaṅga of Anuruddha at MN 127:4.5.
comment_text.sn41.7:1.5: Text ends this sentence with close -ti indicating end of quote, yet throughout the vocative bhante is used, indicating that it is Citta speaking. The particle is absent from the exact parallel at MN 43:30.2, but present in the dialogue at MN 127:5.3, which features more back-and-forth between the speakers.
comment_text.sn41.7:5.2: The suttas say little about this signless (animitta) meditation. Its defining characteristic is that consciousness does not “follow after signs” (nimittānusāri viññāṇaṁ, eg. AN 6.13:5.3). This is explained in MN 138:10.2 as not being distracted or affected by the features of sense impressions, as the “signs” are created by greed, hate, and delusion (MN 43:37.1). It is distinguished from the four kinds of mindfulness meditation (SN 22.80:8.4). The mental unification (ekattaṁ) based on this practice is listed after the form (and formless) jhānas, so it is very advanced. It is nonetheless a conditioned state (MN 121:11.4), so it is possible that a mendicant might fall from it and disrobe (AN 6.60:8.10). However it may also be used to describe the meditation of an arahant (SN 41.7:6.12). It is clear from MN 121:10.5 that the six senses are still functioning, unlike in deep serenity meditations. It seems that in such a state, the Buddha was able to function normally while seeing through the pain in his body (DN 16:2.25.13).

root_text.sn41.7:1.1: Ekaṁ samayaṁ āyasmā godatto macchikāsaṇḍe viharati ambāṭakavane.
root_text.sn41.7:1.2: Atha kho citto gahapati yenāyasmā godatto tenupasaṅkami; upasaṅkamitvā āyasmantaṁ godattaṁ abhivādetvā ekamantaṁ nisīdi. Ekamantaṁ nisinnaṁ kho cittaṁ gahapatiṁ āyasmā godatto etadavoca:
root_text.sn41.7:1.3: “yā cāyaṁ, gahapati, appamāṇā cetovimutti, yā ca ākiñcaññā cetovimutti, yā ca suññatā cetovimutti, yā ca animittā cetovimutti, ime dhammā nānatthā nānābyañjanā udāhu ekatthā byañjanameva nānan”ti?
root_text.sn41.7:1.4: “Atthi, bhante, pariyāyo yaṁ pariyāyaṁ āgamma ime dhammā nānatthā ceva nānābyañjanā ca.
root_text.sn41.7:1.5: Atthi pana, bhante, pariyāyo yaṁ pariyāyaṁ āgamma ime dhammā ekatthā byañjanameva nānan”ti.
root_text.sn41.7:2.1: “Katamo ca, bhante, pariyāyo yaṁ pariyāyaṁ āgamma ime dhammā nānatthā ceva nānābyañjanā ca?
root_text.sn41.7:2.2: Idha, bhante, bhikkhu mettāsahagatena cetasā ekaṁ disaṁ pharitvā viharati, tathā dutiyaṁ, tathā tatiyaṁ, tathā catutthaṁ. Iti uddhamadho tiriyaṁ sabbadhi sabbattatāya sabbāvantaṁ lokaṁ mettāsahagatena cetasā vipulena mahaggatena appamāṇena averena abyāpajjena pharitvā viharati.
root_text.sn41.7:2.5: upekkhāsahagatena cetasā ekaṁ disaṁ pharitvā viharati, tathā dutiyaṁ, tathā tatiyaṁ, tathā catutthaṁ. Iti uddhamadho tiriyaṁ sabbadhi sabbattatāya sabbāvantaṁ lokaṁ upekkhāsahagatena cetasā vipulena mahaggatena appamāṇena averena abyāpajjena pharitvā viharati.
root_text.sn41.7:3.2: Idha, bhante, bhikkhu sabbaso viññāṇañcāyatanaṁ samatikkamma, ‘natthi kiñcī’ti ākiñcaññāyatanaṁ upasampajja viharati.
root_text.sn41.7:4.2: Idha, bhante, bhikkhu araññagato vā rukkhamūlagato vā suññāgāragato vā iti paṭisañcikkhati:
root_text.sn41.7:5.2: Idha, bhante, bhikkhu sabbanimittānaṁ amanasikārā animittaṁ cetosamādhiṁ upasampajja viharati.
root_text.sn41.7:5.4: Ayaṁ kho, bhante, pariyāyo yaṁ pariyāyaṁ āgamma ime dhammā nānatthā ceva nānābyañjanā ca.
root_text.sn41.7:6.1: Katamo ca, bhante, pariyāyo yaṁ pariyāyaṁ āgamma ime dhammā ekatthā byañjanameva nānaṁ?
root_text.sn41.7:6.2: Rāgo, bhante, pamāṇakaraṇo, doso pamāṇakaraṇo, moho pamāṇakaraṇo.
root_text.sn41.7:6.3: Te khīṇāsavassa bhikkhuno pahīnā ucchinnamūlā tālāvatthukatā anabhāvaṅkatā āyatiṁ anuppādadhammā.
root_text.sn41.7:6.4: Yāvatā kho, bhante, appamāṇā cetovimuttiyo, akuppā tāsaṁ cetovimutti aggamakkhāyati.
root_text.sn41.7:6.5: Sā kho pana akuppā cetovimutti suññā rāgena, suññā dosena, suññā mohena.
root_text.sn41.7:6.6: Rāgo kho, bhante, kiñcanaṁ, doso kiñcanaṁ, moho kiñcanaṁ.
root_text.sn41.7:6.7: Te khīṇāsavassa bhikkhuno pahīnā ucchinnamūlā tālāvatthukatā anabhāvaṅkatā āyatiṁ anuppādadhammā.
root_text.sn41.7:6.8: Yāvatā kho, bhante, ākiñcaññā cetovimuttiyo, akuppā tāsaṁ cetovimutti aggamakkhāyati.
root_text.sn41.7:6.9: Sā kho pana akuppā cetovimutti suññā rāgena, suññā dosena, suññā mohena.
root_text.sn41.7:6.10: Rāgo kho, bhante, nimittakaraṇo, doso nimittakaraṇo, moho nimittakaraṇo.
root_text.sn41.7:6.11: Te khīṇāsavassa bhikkhuno pahīnā ucchinnamūlā tālāvatthukatā anabhāvaṅkatā āyatiṁ anuppādadhammā.
root_text.sn41.7:6.12: Yāvatā kho, bhante, animittā cetovimuttiyo, akuppā tāsaṁ cetovimutti aggamakkhāyati.
root_text.sn41.7:6.13: Sā kho pana akuppā cetovimutti suññā rāgena, suññā dosena, suññā mohena.
root_text.sn41.7:6.14: Ayaṁ kho, bhante, pariyāyo yaṁ pariyāyaṁ āgamma ime dhammā ekatthā byañjanameva nānan”ti.
root_text.sn41.7:6.16: Yassa te gambhīre buddhavacane paññācakkhu kamatī”ti.

translation_text.sn41.7:1.1: At one time Venerable Godatta was staying near Macchikāsaṇḍa in the Wild Mango Grove.
translation_text.sn41.7:1.2: Then Citta the householder went up to Venerable Godatta, bowed, and sat down to one side. Godatta said to him:
translation_text.sn41.7:1.3: “Householder, the limitless release of the heart, and the release of the heart through nothingness, and the release of the heart through emptiness, and the signless release of the heart: do these things differ in both meaning and phrasing? Or do they mean the same thing, and differ only in the phrasing?”
translation_text.sn41.7:1.4: “Sir, there is a way in which these things differ in both meaning and phrasing.
translation_text.sn41.7:1.5: But there’s also a way in which they mean the same thing, and differ only in the phrasing.
translation_text.sn41.7:2.1: And what’s the way in which these things differ in both meaning and phrasing?
translation_text.sn41.7:2.2: It’s when a mendicant meditates spreading a heart full of love to one direction, and to the second, and to the third, and to the fourth. In the same way above, below, across, everywhere, all around, they spread a heart full of love to the whole world—abundant, expansive, limitless, free of enmity and ill will.
translation_text.sn41.7:2.3: They meditate spreading a heart full of compassion …
translation_text.sn41.7:2.4: They meditate spreading a heart full of rejoicing …
translation_text.sn41.7:2.5: They meditate spreading a heart full of equanimity to one direction, and to the second, and to the third, and to the fourth. In the same way above, below, across, everywhere, all around, they spread a heart full of equanimity to the whole world—abundant, expansive, limitless, free of enmity and ill will.
translation_text.sn41.7:2.6: This is called the limitless release of the heart.
translation_text.sn41.7:3.1: And what is the release of the heart through nothingness?
translation_text.sn41.7:3.2: It’s when a mendicant, going totally beyond the dimension of infinite consciousness, aware that ‘there is nothing at all’, enters and remains in the dimension of nothingness.
translation_text.sn41.7:3.3: This is called the release of the heart through nothingness.
translation_text.sn41.7:4.1: And what is the release of the heart through emptiness?
translation_text.sn41.7:4.2: It’s when a mendicant has gone to a wilderness, or to the root of a tree, or to an empty hut, and reflects like this:
translation_text.sn41.7:4.3: ‘This is empty of a self or what belongs to a self.’
translation_text.sn41.7:4.4: This is called the release of the heart through emptiness.
translation_text.sn41.7:5.1: And what is the signless heart’s release?
translation_text.sn41.7:5.2: It’s when a mendicant, not focusing on any signs, enters and remains in the signless immersion of the heart.
translation_text.sn41.7:5.3: This is called the signless release of the heart.
translation_text.sn41.7:5.4: This is the way in which these things differ in both meaning and phrasing.
translation_text.sn41.7:6.1: And what’s the way in which they mean the same thing, and differ only in the phrasing?
translation_text.sn41.7:6.2: Greed, hate, and delusion are makers of limits.
translation_text.sn41.7:6.3: A mendicant who has ended the defilements has given these up, cut them off at the root, made them like a palm stump, and obliterated them, so they are unable to arise in the future.
translation_text.sn41.7:6.4: The unshakable release of the heart is said to be the best kind of limitless release of the heart.
translation_text.sn41.7:6.5: That unshakable release of the heart is empty of greed, hate, and delusion.
translation_text.sn41.7:6.6: Greed is something, hate is something, and delusion is something.
translation_text.sn41.7:6.7: A mendicant who has ended the defilements has given these up, cut them off at the root, made them like a palm stump, and obliterated them, so they are unable to arise in the future.
translation_text.sn41.7:6.8: The unshakable release of the heart is said to be the best kind of release of the heart through nothingness.
translation_text.sn41.7:6.9: That unshakable release of the heart is empty of greed, hate, and delusion.
translation_text.sn41.7:6.10: Greed, hate, and delusion are makers of signs.
translation_text.sn41.7:6.11: A mendicant who has ended the defilements has given these up, cut them off at the root, made them like a palm stump, and obliterated them, so they are unable to arise in the future.
translation_text.sn41.7:6.12: The unshakable release of the heart is said to be the best kind of signless release of the heart.
translation_text.sn41.7:6.13: That unshakable release of the heart is empty of greed, hate, and delusion.
translation_text.sn41.7:6.14: This is the way in which they mean the same thing, and differ only in the phrasing.”
translation_text.sn41.7:6.16: to traverse the Buddha’s deep teachings with the eye of wisdom.”

variant_text.sn41.7:2.2: catutthaṁ → catutthiṁ (bj) | abyāpajjena → abyāpajjhena (bj, sya-all, km); avyāpajjhena (pts1ed); abyābajjhena (?)
